title: Green-wood stool leg
subtitle: A round-section stool leg drawknifed and spokeshaved from riven green ash
excerpt: The stool leg is the fundamental unit of green-woodwork chair and seat construction. A riven ash billet, drawknifed to a rough cylinder and spokeshaved to final dimension on the shaving horse, produces a leg that is stronger than any machined equivalent because the grain runs continuously along its length. The same process produces chair legs, stretchers, and rake handles.
sourceNotes: Green-woodwork chair and stool leg construction from riven ash documented in British chairmaking traditions (Chiltern chair-bodging) and in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

content.text: Chair bodgers in the Chiltern Hills made millions of
ash legs by the same method:
it to a rough shape, then
to the final size. A green ash leg pushed into a seasoned seat shrinks onto the
hole as it dries. The joint needs no glue and stays tight with use.

Green ash billets, riven from a straight-grained section of trunk or large branch, each slightly longer than the finished leg dimension. A typical three-legged stool leg is 42 to 45 cm long; the billet should be 50 cm to allow for end trimming. The billet should be roughly square in section at about 5 cm per side. Birch and elm are alternative species; both rive and shape well, though elm can be more variable in grain.

A froe and beetle mallet for riving (or an axe if the froe is not available). A drawknife and shaving horse for roughing to cylinder. A spokeshave for finishing to final dimension and forming the tenon. A leather strop for both blades. A waterstone for the spokeshave if it needs honing during work.

to rive the leg blank from a larger billet. The grain must run straight along the full length. Any deviation in grain will show up as a tendency for the leg to bow as it dries; select a billet with clean straight grain.
Clamp the billet on the shaving horse with the tenon end toward you. Use the

to remove the four corners of the roughly square section, working from the tenon end toward the foot end. Then take the eight resulting corners off to produce a sixteen-sided section. Check diameter with calipers: the target for a stool leg is 32 to 35 mm at mid-shaft. Stop drawknife work when 3 to 4 mm above this dimension; the spokeshave takes it to final.

The spokeshave is pushed away from the body along the length of the leg, still clamped on the shaving horse. Set the blade depth for a fine shaving; too deep and the spokeshave will chatter or dig. Work in long strokes from one end to the other, rotating the leg a quarter turn after every full pass. Check diameter with calipers at three points along the length: the leg should be straight and even in section.

The tenon end of the leg is reduced in diameter to fit a drilled bore in the seat. A typical stool tenon is 22 to 25 mm in diameter at its widest point, tapering over 30 to 40 mm of length. Shape the tenon with the spokeshave, checking against the bore diameter frequently. The green tenon should be cut to final dimension at the tenon: as the leg dries, it will shrink to produce a tight fit in the seasoned seat bore.

Stand finished legs upright in a warm room for at least seven days before fitting. The tenon end will shrink slightly; if the fit was correct when green, it will be very slightly loose when dry. This is correct: the friction of assembly and any wedging will take up the slack.
Stool legs receive no finish in the green-woodwork tradition. A light oil on the shaft after assembly and full drying is optional.
Make a set of three for a three-legged milking stool. This is the simplest and most stable shape. A three-point base always sits flat, so the leg angles don't need to match exactly.
Use the same process to make stretchers (horizontal rails between legs): thinner in section (20 to 22 mm) and shorter, with a tenon at each end.

items.fix: Accept the bow if it is minor; a slight bow in a stool leg is traditional. For future work, sight along the billet before riving to verify straight grain, and reject billets with visible deviation.
items.cause: The grain in the billet was not perfectly straight along the length; one face dried faster than the other because of grain deviation.
items.symptom: The leg bows along its length after drying

items.fix: Retract the blade one half-turn. Strop the blade. Take a lighter test pass; if smooth, continue at that setting.
items.cause: The blade is set too deep or is dull; the wood is resisting rather than shaving.

items.fix: Drive a thin hardwood wedge into a saw cut across the diameter of the tenon end and trim. Alternatively, wrap the tenon in a thin layer of hide glue and paper before assembly.
items.cause: The tenon was cut too fine when green; the subsequent shrinkage made it undersized.
items.symptom: The tenon is too small for the bore after drying
